title: Te Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku / Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will investigate the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our college grounds and holds significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The awa is named for the native kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that once flourished along its banks, and students will use mathematical analysis to support restoration efforts.
learningObjectives: Calculate and compare water quality metrics (pH, turbidity, temperature) using appropriate units of measurement
Create and interpret line graphs showing seasonal changes in stream health indicators
Apply geometric principles to calculate the surface area and volume of the stream for habitat assessment
Use statistical measures (mean, median, mode) to analyze biodiversity data collected from the stream ecosystem

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Begin at the stream with a karakia and introduction to its significance. Students make initial observations and sketch the stream's path, estimating lengths and angles using protractors and measuring tapes.

activities.title: Inenga Wai Māori - Water Quality Measurement
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students collect and record data on pH levels, temperature, and turbidity at three different points along the stream. They convert between metric units and calculate averages.

activities.title: Hanga Tauaromahi - Creating Data Visualizations
activities.description: Students create line graphs and bar charts showing their collected data over time. They analyze patterns and correlations between different water quality indicators.

activities.title: Te Tātari Mataara - Geometric Analysis
activities.description: Using cross-sectional measurements, students calculate the stream's volume and surface area to understand habitat capacity and potential for kotukutuku restoration planting.

activities.title: Te Whakatau Take - Data-Driven Decision Making
activities.description: Groups present their findings and propose specific restoration actions based on mathematical evidence, creating scaled planting plans for the stream banks.

resources: Local kaumātua Hone Smith (Ngāti Kahungunu environmental knowledge holder)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council water monitoring toolkit and data sets
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit'
Mangakōtukutuku College's historical stream health records and maps

tasks: Complete a stream health data collection and analysis workbook
Create a professional-quality infographic presenting key findings
Present a 3-minute pechakucha proposing evidence-based restoration actions

nzcAlignment: Mathematics and Statistics: Statistical investigation; Measurement; Number strategies
Science: Living World; Planet Earth and Beyond
